chartPlan.title: India's population, 1960 to today
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The headline count and the shape of long-term growth.
chartPlan.howToRead: Look at the line rising from left to right; note the steepening slope and recent slowing.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Don't assume the line tells you about government counts—it's based on estimates.

chartPlan.title: India, China and the world
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Puts India's size in global context and shows it overtook China.
chartPlan.howToRead: India's line overtakes China's around 2023; the world line is on a different scale.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Don't read the absolute levels without checking the y-axis scales—India and China series are similar, world is much larger.

chartPlan.title: India's share of world population
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Shows what fraction of humanity is Indian over time.
chartPlan.howToRead: The line trends upward, now about 17.8%.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: A small percentage change in share means millions of people because the world population is large.

chartPlan.title: Where Indians live: the slow shift to cities
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Urbanisation is slow but affects fertility and economic structure.
chartPlan.howToRead: The line rises slowly from 17.9% in 1960 to 35.4% in 2024.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Don't interpret urban share as absolute urban population—the absolute number has grown much faster.

chartPlan.title: Annual population growth rate, to 2100
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Shows when growth turns negative and the scenario spread.
chartPlan.howToRead: The median line crosses zero around 2061; the fan shows high, low, and other variants.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: A slowing growth rate still means large annual additions for many years.

chartPlan.title: Births per 1,000 people
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Latest Indian data on the flow of new births.
chartPlan.howToRead: All three lines (All India, Rural, Urban) declining; urban is lowest.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Crude birth rate is affected by age structure, not just fertility.

chartPlan.title: Births per woman, 1960 to today
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The long arc of fertility decline is the engine of demographic change.
chartPlan.howToRead: TFR dropped from 5.92 in 1960 to 1.96 in 2024.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Don't confuse TFR with the number of births in a single year.

chartPlan.title: Fertility is now below replacement
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Confirms that India has tipped below the replacement level of 2.1.
chartPlan.howToRead: The all-India line falls below 2.1; urban is much lower, rural is at replacement.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Below-replacement fertility doesn't mean immediate population decline due to momentum.

chartPlan.title: Fertility by state
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Reveals the sharp north-south divide in fertility.
chartPlan.howToRead: Use the colour scale: deep colours indicate higher rates; notice the cluster of high rates in the north.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: A state with high TFR now might rapidly fall in a few years.

chartPlan.title: Highest and lowest fertility states
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Makes the ranking explicit and highlights extremes.
chartPlan.howToRead: Top five (Bihar 2.7 to Rajasthan 2.1) vs bottom five (Andaman 0.9 to Punjab 1.6).
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Rankings can change with next survey; small differences tighten over time.

chartPlan.title: At what age Indian women have children
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Shows the pattern of childbearing by age.
chartPlan.howToRead: Peak at 25-29 (134.7 births per 1,000 women), low at extremes.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: The bars sum to TFR per woman, not total births.

chartPlan.title: Women are having children later
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Tracks the rising mean age at childbearing.
chartPlan.howToRead: Mean age rose from 25.48 in 2000 to 27.53 projected for 2030 and beyond.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: A higher mean age doesn't mean fewer children total, but it typically accompanies lower fertility.

chartPlan.title: How India avoids pregnancy, and who carries it
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Exposes the gender imbalance in contraception.
chartPlan.howToRead: Female sterilisation dominates (36.5%); male sterilisation is negligible (0.5%).
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: The total modern method use (52.7%) includes female and male sterilisation, not just one.

chartPlan.title: Families are getting smaller
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Confirms the shift toward two-child families.
chartPlan.howToRead: 66.4% of births are first children; only 3.5% are fourth or higher.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: The percentages reflect current births, not completed family size of older women.

chartPlan.title: Marrying and giving birth young
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Shows the persistence of early marriage and teen motherhood.
chartPlan.howToRead: 20.1% of women 20-24 married before 18; 6.7% of 15-19 are mothers or pregnant.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: These are national averages; some states have much higher rates.

chartPlan.title: Fertility falls with schooling
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Demonstrates the strong gradient of education on fertility.
chartPlan.howToRead: TFR drops from 2.82 for no schooling to 1.78 for 12+ years of schooling.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Education and wealth are correlated; this is not a pure causal effect.

chartPlan.title: Fertility falls with wealth
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Shows that the poorest have the highest fertility.
chartPlan.howToRead: TFR goes from 2.63 in the lowest wealth quintile to 1.57 in the highest.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Wealth and education measures overlap considerably.

chartPlan.title: Fertility by religion
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Addresses the most misread fertility number in India.
chartPlan.howToRead: Muslims have highest TFR (2.36), Hindus (1.94), Buddhists lowest (1.39); all are declining.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Do not interpret these as permanent gaps; convergence is underway.

chartPlan.title: Boys per girl at birth: the son-preference signal
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Quantifies the sex ratio imbalance.
chartPlan.howToRead: National SRB is 918 girls per 1,000 boys (2022-24), up from 907 earlier.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: A ratio below 950 is considered distorted due to sex-selective practices.

chartPlan.title: India's age pyramid today
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Visualises the entire age and sex structure.
chartPlan.howToRead: The pyramid is broad at the base but narrowing; the 2030 projection shows a still-young structure with a large working-age bulge.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Don't mistake this for a count; it's a snapshot of one year.

chartPlan.title: The age mix is shifting
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Tracks the three age shares over time.
chartPlan.howToRead: Children's share falling, working-age peaking, elderly rising.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: The working-age share peaking is a demographic dividend, but it's temporary.

chartPlan.title: Median age, to 2100
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Summarises ageing in one number.
chartPlan.howToRead: Median age rises from 21.2 in 2000 to 47.8 by 2100.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Median age can mask the size of the elderly population.

chartPlan.title: Old-age dependency ratio
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Shows the burden on workers.
chartPlan.howToRead: In 2030, about 12.4 elderly per 100 working-age (15-64).
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Dependency ratios don't account for who actually works or receives care.

chartPlan.title: Indians are living longer
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Life expectancy is the other engine of ageing.
chartPlan.howToRead: Life expectancy rose from 45.6 years in 1960 to 72.2 years in 2024.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Life expectancy at birth is an average, not a guarantee.

chartPlan.title: Share of Indians aged 65 and over
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Makes ageing tangible as a population share.
chartPlan.howToRead: The elderly share rose from 3.3% in 1960 to 7.1% in 2024, and will keep rising.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: A small share change in a large population means millions more elderly.

chartPlan.title: Income per person as India ages
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Contextualises the 'old before rich' concern.
chartPlan.howToRead: GDP per capita was $85 in 1960, $2,695 in 2024, still far below rich countries when they aged.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: GDP per capita is an average; most Indians have lower incomes.

chartPlan.title: India's population to 2100: the peak
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The payoff: projection of the peak and decline.
chartPlan.howToRead: The median line peaks around 2061 near 1.70 billion, then declines to 1.51 billion by 2100.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: The peak is not a certainty; it depends on future fertility.

chartPlan.title: How high, how soon? Scenarios to 2100
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Shows the fan of possibilities.
chartPlan.howToRead: High-fertility scenario grows to 2.2 billion, low-fertility declines below 1 billion.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: The constant-fertility scenario is unrealistic because fertility already fell below that.

chartPlan.title: The models disagree on the peak
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Highlights uncertainty between UN and IHME.
chartPlan.howToRead: UN peaks later and higher; IHME peaks around 2048 at 1.61 billion and falls to 1.09 billion.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Both are projections, not predictions; real fertility could differ.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: Think of a group of women who are teenagers today. If they had babies at the rates we see in each age group right now, the total number of children they'd have over their whole lives is the TFR. It's not how many children they actually have, but a snapshot of current birth rates. A TFR of 2 means the average woman would have two children in her lifetime if rates stay the same.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: TFR is the key number for understanding whether India's population will grow or shrink in the long run. The replacement level is about 2.1, and India's TFR is now below that.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: Imagine a couple replaces themselves with two children. But some children may not survive to have their own children, so we need a little more than two. Roughly 2.1 children per woman is the level that keeps the population steady in the long run (without migration).
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: India's TFR dropping below 2.1 signals that without migration, the population will eventually stop growing and start to decline, though it takes decades because of momentum.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: Think of a fully loaded truck moving on a highway. Even if the driver lifts their foot off the accelerator, the truck doesn't stop immediately; it keeps rolling for a while. Similarly, even after fertility falls below replacement, a large generation already born in the past keeps having children, so the total population continues to grow for decades before it starts shrinking.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: Momentum is why India is still adding large numbers of people even though birth rates per woman have fallen. It explains the gap between fertility decline and population decline.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: If you line up everyone in India from youngest to oldest, the median age is the age of the person standing exactly in the middle. Half the population is older, half younger. It's a quick way to see how young or old a country is.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: India's median age is rising from about 21 to nearly 48 by 2100, showing a dramatic shift from a young to an ageing society.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: Think of this like a family: it's the number of grandparents (people 65+) compared to the number of working-age adults (15-64). If there are 12 grandparents for every 100 working-age family members, that's a 12% old-age dependency ratio. The higher the ratio, the greater the potential burden on the middle generation to provide support.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: India's old-age dependency ratio is projected to rise sharply, which will challenge social support systems, especially as the country ages at a relatively low income level.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: Take the total value of everything the country produces in a year (GDP) and divide by the population. It gives a rough average income per person. But it's just an average; many people earn much less, and a few earn much more.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: India's GDP per capita is around $2,695. This is far lower than the levels at which wealthy countries aged, fueling concerns about India getting old before it gets rich, meaning it may have fewer resources to support its elderly.

No outside facts; use only the provided evidence.
No calculations unless the evidence packet supplies the derived number.

selectionRules: Start from the page question, not from the indicator name.
Choose the smallest set of visuals that explains scale, trend, structure, comparison, and caveat.
Prefer mobile-friendly charts: line charts with sparse ticks, horizontal bars, share strips, pyramids only when essential, and no dense tables.
Use one strong pull quote only when the evidence supports it with a number.
Add glossary blocks for technical terms that normal readers may not know.
caveats: Use only figures present in lockedNumbers or sourceSummaries.
If a related theme indicator is present, you may use it for context, but do not pretend it is the main answer.
If source coverage is thin, say so directly.
Do not compare states, districts, or cities unless the evidence packet contains those geographies.
forbiddenClaims: Do not infer causality from a trend unless the evidence explicitly supports it.
Do not cite a source that is not present in sourceSummaries.
